speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Yeah, I think you're thinking about it the right way Pierre and the other thing which I'll share is that, you know, you can't just look at one quarter versus the other quarter in terms of churn, but we are actually seeing churn of subscribers also coming down,
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: which again is the reflection of the product is getting better.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And obviously if subscriptions are going up that is a good metric. The other thing also to note is that we are seeing customers actually drive longer, which again you could correlate it.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: That's why you have lesser churn because people are liking the product.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And if I mean, I've said this before, if I just use my own personal behavior, right, I literally get in the car, I press the button and it just goes and earlier I used to park.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Now I don't even have to park. And that is the experience which we want everybody to get.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And that's why you're starting seeing it in the numbers come through.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And if I made maybe a quick follow completely different it's more on the optimus architecture.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And you talked about the partnership with XAI and and Greg and I was wondering if you can share with us anything about how the system to.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Intelligence is going to be implemented is going to be on board on ships inside optimus or if we should think about like your fleet of like a million of them as being produced a year.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Actually driving very significant inference demand in data centers as well for system to thinking.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: We think we can put a lot of intelligence locally in the robot and and certainly needs to be enough intelligence that the robot gets disconnected like if it's a bad cellular signal.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: There isn't Wi-Fi, you know, optimus can't just get stuck.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: It needs to have enough local intelligence that it can still do useful things.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Even if it loses connection kind of like the car, the car does not need any cellular Wi-Fi connection to be able to drive safely.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Now I guess you can think of like optimus needs kind of a manager to tell what to do at the broadly speaking like if you know otherwise it's going to keep doing the same thing it did before.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So, you know, I think you need kind of an orchestration AI, which, you know, GARC would be good for orchestration.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And then for, you know, for optimus's voice, you know, having a low latency intelligent voice AI, GARC is actually very good for that.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So if you want to talk to optimus and have kind of a, you know, a GARC level conversation, you kind of need to connect to a GARC level AI for that.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: But, but I would expect the amount of interaction apart from like, you know, the voice, voice stuff and asking complicated questions of the robot that necessarily needs a large AI model to answer.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: The, you know, GARC would probably have about as much interaction with optimus as a manager would have with the people on their team.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So meaning, meaning optimus could probably work for several hours without any management oversight.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Great. The next question is going to come from Dan at Barclays. Dan, please feel free to unmute yourself.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Great. Good evening. Thank you for taking questions.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Elon, your chip suppliers generally generate pretty good economics on the chip they sell.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Your approach has historically been on vertical integration. Part of that has been to get better economics.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So I know the longer term goal of TerraFAB is to get the supply you need, but how much of TerraFAB is also motivated to get better economics on your own?
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Mid term chip purchases and how long is it going to take to ramp to get to a yield that achieves that type of economic parity?
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: No, I mean, TerraFAB is not some sort of mechanism to generate leverage over our chip suppliers.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: It's just literally we don't see a path to having enough.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: The efficient quantity of AI chips down the road as we scale production to high levels.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Just the rate at which the industry is growing in logic, but even more so in memory, it just doesn't, you know, we just anticipate hitting the wall if we don't make chips ourselves.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So that's the reason for the TerraFAB. I think that we do have some ideas for how to make maybe radically better AI chips.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And these are kind of research ideas there, you know, which means like long shot, but if long shot pays off, it's maybe a giant improvement.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And it's just easier to do that if we have our own research lab and our developing our own production technologies.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So, and if you look sort of long term at, you know, say having AI satellites making chips for those, there's just no way in hell the existing industry can keep up with that.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: It's impossible.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: All right. And our next question is going to come from Mark at Goldman Sachs. Mark, please feel free to unmute yourself.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Yes, good afternoon. Thank you very much for taking my question.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Recognizing the importance of FSD and that FSD can help to drive vehicle sales and nice to see some of the improvements in the FSD technology more recently with version 14.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: However, I'm also hoping to understand if the company's view on new vehicle models has evolved and ask, given that you posted on axe recently that Tesla could develop a family vehicle.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: There's also been some past discussion about a compact vehicle.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Well, I mean, cyber cap is the compact vehicle. It's actually, I mean, it's very roomy, but it's a two person vehicle.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And we do think probably most of our production long term will be cyber cap because 90% of miles driven are with two of one or two people.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So it would mean that, you know, you'd want a vast majority of your production to be cyber cap.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Then over time, it's going to make sense for our whole lineup to be autonomous vehicles of different sizes.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And I did talk a bit about this when we did the AI day in LA at Warner Brothers and showed like, this is our current lineup.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And this is what, you know, some idea of what our future lineup will be, which is that it's going to be almost entirely autonomous.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: In fact, long term, the only manually driven car will be the new Tesla Roadster.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Speaking of which, we may be able to debut that in a month or so.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: It requires a lot of testing and validation before we can actually have a demo and not have something go wrong with a demo.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: But I think it will be one of the most exciting product on bales ever.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: I'm not sure. I don't think it moves the needle massively from a revenue standpoint.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: But it is very cool.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: I think it might be one of the most spectacular demos ever.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: All right, Mark, did you have a follow up question?
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Yeah, thanks Travis.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: My other question was on batteries and the company mentioned batteries as a constraint on its growth.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Can you speak more to how Tesla expects to resolve this and to what extent that might come from ramping up your own LFP and 4680 battery cell manufacturing?
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Or is it something that you'd expect to resolve primarily with increased sourcing from suppliers? Thank you.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Yeah, so at the moment, I think the limiter is not the cells itself.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: It's the battery pack capacity.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And you know, like I said in my opening remarks, we're actively working on resolving this.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: There's more capacity being added as we speak and I'll let Lars add a few more thanks to it.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Thanks, very well.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: As you guys may have seen in Berlin, we started launching a Model Y battery pack with our in-house 4680 cells
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: ago and that is ramping up nicely adding to Berlin's output and helping with the demand surge that we've seen in Europe.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: As well, we're adding additional capacity in our Reno facility sort of retooling it as it's been building packs now for almost 10 years.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And in order to put in some more efficient lines and get additional output out there.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And then we continue to have growth in China as well, ramping in-house LFP module production and battery packs associated with that.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So all of those things are happening now and in the next months and that's really plans we laid out a few months back to increase that output with the growing demand.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: All right, thank you guys.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And our next analyst is going to be Colin from Wells Fargo.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Colin, please feel free to unmute yourself.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Oh, great. Thanks for taking my questions.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: You moved to the safety driver in Austin and you're now expanding into Allison Houston.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: What are the key safety metrics that you're tracking that gives you confidence that robo taxi is safe in us to expand as it sort of miles per intervention, miles per accident per fatality and where do you stand on that now?
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Sure.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Yeah.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: We track basically all the metrics that you mentioned. We have a pretty large QA fleet spread across all of the United States.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And then we look at any intervention that could happen and then sort of simulate both in practice and also in our simulators that are very, very good nowadays using neural networks as what would have happened.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And then based on all these analysis in the end, make the call to expand.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And so far, all of the expansions have gone according to our expectations.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Yeah, a lot of the limiting, a lot of what limits wider deployment of robo taxi are actually not safety issues, but convenience issues or the car basically gets paranoid and get stuck.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Like sometimes it gets, because it's program for maximum safety. So the problem is that then it sometimes just gets scared to do things.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So like sometimes gets scared to cross railroads, for example, or it'll get stuck at a light where the light never changes from red or, I mean, there was one kind of amusing situation where a whole bunch of
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: robo taxis got stuck in the left turn lane in Austin because a kid did not, a way more had crashed into a bus.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And so they could not turn left because the way more crashed into the bus. And so you had this like long line of like, I don't know, it does no more.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: It tells the robo taxis that we're waiting for the bus to move, but the bus was never going to move because the way more is crashed into the bus.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So that obviously drives people crazy if there's a whole bunch of robo taxis blocking the whole road.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So it's a ton of things like that. That's the single biggest thing is just the car being scared to move or getting kind of stuck in situations like that.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: We've also had literal infinite loops where the car might want to make a turn into a road, but there's construction.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And then it goes around the block tries to turn into the road to construction goes around the block tries to turn the road.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And so you have to stop the infinite looping, the literal infinite looping.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So those are actually, those are by far the issues that we have to resolve as opposed to direct safety issues.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: I'm not a great and then did you follow up?
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Yeah, just last year I asked about, you know, FSD and camera and the issues with sung layer and you noted that there was a breakthrough with direct photon counting that address this issue.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: But a month ago, there was a nits of filing saying that they haven't received an update when the solution was deployed and the number of vehicles.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Is this to, did it require a retrofit of the camera? Is this fully deployed? I guess we're just curious since the filing mentioned it.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Yeah, first I want to say we did a change in cameras some months ago and those are out and the nits of flying is referring to like older vehicles.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: We always work directly with nits on all of the issues that they raised with us and there's, they're asking for quite a bit of information and, you know, we're complying with that.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: And it is timely manner as possible. And so we expect to resolve that and any of the other investigations in short order.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Yeah, and we have also implemented shifter measures for the, um, the visibility of the camera.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: So in recent softribbles, if the camera is not able to see things clearly because of, you know, residue, build up or what have you, then the FSD won't be available for those cars.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: It just needs to have to clean the inside of the windscreen.
speaker: TRAVIS AXELROD - HEAD OF INVESTOR RELATIONS

text: Great. That unfortunately is all the time we have today. We appreciate everyone's questions and we look forward to talking to you next quarter. Thank you very much and goodbye.
